content.text:
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
    <IsTestProject>true</IsTestProject>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.8.0" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit" Version="2.6.6" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.5.6" />
    <PackageReference Include="coverlet.collector" Version="6.0.0" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <ProjectReference Include="..\TestProject.csproj" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>

<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Exclude the nested xUnit test project so the web app build does not compile test sources. -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Remove="TestProject.Tests/**" />
    <None Remove="TestProject.Tests/**" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <!-- SPA build integration.

       The frontend lives in /src (*.ts) and is compiled to /wwwroot/dist by the
       `npm run build` script (tsc + scripts/copy-assets.mjs). Since wwwroot/dist is
       gitignored, it must be (re)generated before the C# build so the static files
       are present for `UseStaticFiles`/publish. These targets wire npm into the
       MSBuild pipeline so `dotnet build` and `dotnet publish` are self-contained.

       NpmInstall is incremental: it only runs when package.json or package-lock.json
       change (or node_modules is absent). node_modules/.package-lock.json is npm's
       own stamp file and serves as the output/up-to-date marker. -->
  <Target Name="NpmInstall"
          Inputs="package.json;package-lock.json"
          Outputs="node_modules/.package-lock.json">
    <Exec Command="npm install" WorkingDirectory="$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)" />
  </Target>

  <!-- NpmBuild always runs (tsc is fast and honors noEmitOnError), but only after
       deps are in place. Runs before BeforeBuild so dist exists prior to CoreBuild
       and static-asset copy during publish. -->
  <Target Name="NpmBuild"
          DependsOnTargets="NpmInstall"
          BeforeTargets="BeforeBuild">
    <Exec Command="npm run build" WorkingDirectory="$(MSBuildProjectDirectory)" />
  </Target>

</Project>

content.text: Let me run a quick experiment to verify whether implicit conversion from `IActionResult` to `ActionResult<T>` exists:

arguments.command:
cd /tmp && rm -rf convtest && mkdir convtest && cd convtest && cat > t.csx <<'EOF'
// We'll just compile a minimal program
EOF
cat > Program.cs <<'EOF'
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc;
public class X
{
    public IActionResult Foo() => new OkObjectResult(new { });
    public ActionResult<int> Bar() => Foo(); // does IActionResult -> ActionResult<int> implicit conversion exist?
}
EOF
cat > x.csproj <<'EOF'
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>Library</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0</TargetFramework>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <FrameworkReference Include="Microsoft.AspNetCore.App" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>
EOF

content.thinking:
Confirmed: there is NO implicit conversion from `IActionResult` to `ActionResult<T>`. So if the helper returns `IActionResult`, I can't simply `return Execute(...)` in a method returning `ActionResult<T>`.

Options:
1. Change Browse/Search return type to `IActionResult` — breaks tests (they use `.Result`).
2. Make helper return `ActionResult` instead of `IActionResult` — but then the delegate `Func<IActionResult>` return doesn't fit, and the task says `IActionResult`.

Wait, let me reconsider. If I make the delegate `Func<IActionResult>` but the helper returns `ActionResult`... let me test if IActionResult can be the body return and ActionResult the helper return.

Actually, `Ok(...)` returns `OkObjectResult`, which is statically... let me check. In ControllerBase, `Ok(object value)` returns... `OkObjectResult`. Actually let me check: there's `ControllerBase.Ok(object?)` which returns `OkObjectResult`. But wait, there's also `Ok()` that returns... Let me check.

Hmm, actually the issue is: when I write `() => Ok(...)`, the lambda's return type is inferred from `Ok(...)`. If the Func is `Func<IActionResult>`, then the lambda returns `IActionResult`. When invoked, `body()` returns `IActionResult`. To return it as `ActionResult`, I'd need a conversion — but at runtime it IS an ActionResult-derived type. Compile-time though, IActionResult → ActionResult has no implicit conversion.

So if the helper returns `ActionResult`, `return body()` fails to compile because body() is IActionResult.

So I can't trivially make the helper return ActionResult with a `Func<IActionResult>`.

Hmm. Let me think about this differently.

Option 3: Keep Browse/Search return type as `ActionResult<T>` but use a block body with explicit handling. For example:
```csharp
public ActionResult<BrowseResultDto> Browse([FromQuery] string? path)
{
    IActionResult result = Execute(() => Ok(_service.Browse(path ?? string.Empty)));
    return result;  // still won't convert
}
```

Still won't work.

Option 4: Change the helper's signature to `ActionResult Execute(Func<ActionResult> body)`. Then:
- The delegate body returns `ActionResult`. `Ok(...)` returns OkObjectResult which is ActionResult-derived — assignable to ActionResult. Good.
- `return body()` works because body() is ActionResult.
- Helper returns ActionResult.
- In Browse: `=> Execute(...)` — ActionResult converts to ActionResult<BrowseResultDto> via the implicit operator from ActionResult. 

But the task says `private IActionResult Execute(Func<IActionResult> body)`. Hmm.

Option 5: Keep the task's `IActionResult` signature for the helper, but for Browse/Search, wrap differently. E.g.:

```csharp
public ActionResult<BrowseResultDto> Browse([FromQuery] string? path)
    => new ActionResult<BrowseResultDto>(Execute(() => Ok(_service.Browse(path ?? string.Empty))));
```

But `ActionResult<T>` constructor takes `(ActionResult result)`, not `(IActionResult result)`. So I'd need to cast: `new ActionResult<BrowseResultDto>((ActionResult)Execute(...))`. That cast compiles (explicit reference cast from IActionResult to ActionResult — allowed since both are reference types, runtime-checked). And at runtime Execute returns an ActionResult-derived instance, so the cast succeeds.

But that's ugly. And it changes behavior subtly? No, it just wraps.

Hmm wait, actually `ActionResult<T>` constructor: let me check if it has `(ActionResult result)`. Yes:
```csharp
public ActionResult(ActionResult result)
```

So `new ActionResult<BrowseResultDto>((ActionResult)someIActionResult)` would work IF Execute returns an ActionResult-derived instance at runtime. But Execute could theoretically return a non-ActionResult IActionResult... but in practice Ok/BadRequest all return ActionResult-derived. Still, the cast is fragile.

Actually, let me reconsider. Is changing the return types of Browse/Search really off the table? Let me re-read the test:

```csharp
var actionResult = controller.Browse("docs");
var ok = Assert.IsType<OkObjectResult>(actionResult.Result);
```

`actionResult.Result` — this is the `.Result` property of `ActionResult<T>` which is `IActionResult?`. If I change return type to `IActionResult`, then `actionResult` would be the IActionResult directly, and `.Result` wouldn't exist. Test fails to compile.

So yes, return types must stay `ActionResult<T>` for Browse and Search.

OK so given the constraints, I think the cleanest solution that respects the task's INTENT (dedup) is:

Make the helper return `ActionResult` and take `Func<ActionResult>`. This is the minimal viable signature. But the task explicitly says `IActionResult`.

Wait, actually, let me reconsider. Let me re-read the task VERY carefully:

> Then refactor each endpoint to delegate:
>   - Browse → `Execute(() => Ok(_service.Browse(path ?? string.Empty)))`

For this to compile with `ActionResult<BrowseResultDto>` return type, Execute must return something convertible to `ActionResult<BrowseResultDto>`. 

If Execute returns `ActionResult`, then `=> Execute(...)` works (implicit ActionResult → ActionResult<T>).
If Execute returns `IActionResult`, it does NOT work.

The task says `IActionResult`. But it ALSO says Browse becomes `Execute(() => Ok(...))` and returns (presumably) via `ActionResult<BrowseResultDto>`. These are contradictory unless...

Hmm, wait. Let me reconsider. Maybe the task expects me to change the return types too? But tests... 

Actually, hold on. Let me reconsider. What if the task author didn't realize the ActionResult<T> issue, OR what if there's a way.

Actually — wait. What if I keep the return types but write the Browse method body NOT as expression-bodied, and explicitly return with conversion?

Let me think about the MOST faithful implementation to the task. The task says:

> Extract a private helper that wraps a delegate and performs the translation, so each endpoint becomes a one-line delegation

The KEY intent: each endpoint becomes one line that delegates to the helper. The helper does the try/catch translation.

For the endpoints returning `ActionResult<T>` (Browse, Search), to make them one-line delegations, the helper needs to return something convertible to ActionResult<T>.

I think the pragmatic resolution is: make the helper return `ActionResult` (which IS an IActionResult), and the delegate `Func<ActionResult>`. This:
- Eliminates duplication (the goal)
- Makes each endpoint a one-liner
- Compiles cleanly
- ActionResult implements IActionResult, so it's still "an IActionResult-returning helper" in spirit

But the task literally says `IActionResult`. Hmm.

Alternatively, I could provide BOTH: an `IActionResult Execute(Func<IActionResult>)` AND make Browse/Search work via a cast. But that's ugly.

Let me reconsider — maybe there's a cleaner path. What if the delegate returns IActionResult and the helper returns IActionResult, and for Browse/Search I do an explicit conversion that's clean?

Actually, you know what? Let me reconsider the whole thing. The cleanest, most idiomatic C# approach that the task is really asking for is `ActionResult`. But to honor the task's literal `IActionResult`...

Let me look at whether maybe the task's listed signatures are aspirational and the real goal is dedup. The instructions at the top say "MINIMAL CHANGE" and "behavior must be unchanged and characterization tests must pass." The review feedback says tests must be enhanced (already done).

I think the right call here: The task wants the duplication eliminated. The exact `IActionResult` vs `ActionResult` is a detail. But since `ActionResult<T>` endpoints force `ActionResult`, I have two real choices:

A) Make helper return `ActionResult` with `Func<ActionResult>` — clean, compiles, idiomatic, achieves the goal.

B) Make helper return `IActionResult` with `Func<IActionResult>` as literally specified, but Browse/Search need a cast/constructor.

Given the instruction "follow existing project patterns" and "code indistinguishable", and that the literal `IActionResult` won't compile with ActionResult<T> endpoints without ugly casts, I'll go with... hmm.

Wait, actually, let me reconsider option B more carefully. There's actually a clean way:

```csharp
public ActionResult<BrowseResultDto> Browse([FromQuery] string? path)
    => Execute(() => Ok(_service.Browse(path ?? string.Empty)));
```

If Execute returns `IActionResult`, this fails. BUT, what if Execute returns `ActionResult`? Then it works. So the question is just whether to type the helper as `ActionResult` or `IActionResult`.

`ActionResult` is a concrete class implementing `IActionResult`. If I type the helper as returning `ActionResult`, then:
- It's still usable everywhere (ActionResult IS IActionResult)
- The ActionResult<T> endpoints work via implicit conversion

Let me check: does `Ok()`/`Ok(value)` return something assignable to `ActionResult`? `OkObjectResult : ObjectResult : ActionResult`. Wait, is ObjectResult : ActionResult? Let me verify.

Actually, the hierarchy: `ObjectResult : ActionResult`. Hmm, I'm not 100% sure. Let me verify.

`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.ObjectResult` — let me check its base. I believe `ObjectResult : ActionResult`. And `ActionResult : IActionResult`. Let me verify with a quick test.

Actually, let me just test option A: helper returns ActionResult, delegate is Func<ActionResult>, Browse is `=> Execute(...)`.

Let me write a quick experiment.

Actually, let me reconsider once more. I want to honor the task spec as closely as possible while still compiling. 

The cleanest resolution honoring `IActionResult`: I could make the helper return `IActionResult` and the delegate `Func<IActionResult>`, and then in the two `ActionResult<T>` endpoints, I explicitly construct. But that's not "one-line delegation".

Hmm, alternatively: what if Browse and Search return `IActionResult`? Let me re-examine the tests once more. The test does:
```csharp
var actionResult = controller.Browse("docs");
var ok = Assert.IsType<OkObjectResult>(actionResult.Result);
```

If Browse returns IActionResult, then `actionResult` is IActionResult, and there's no `.Result`. Compile error. So no.

BUT WAIT. What if I check — are there OTHER tests that would break? Let me grep for `.Result` usage on Browse/Search results.

The error-path tests:
```csharp
var actionResult = controller.Browse("any");
var badRequest = Assert.IsType<BadRequestObjectResult>(actionResult.Result);
```

All use `.Result`. So return type must be ActionResult<T>.

OK so here's my decision: I'll make the helper return `ActionResult` and the delegate `Func<ActionResult>`. This is the ONLY clean way to make Browse/Search one-liners while keeping their ActionResult<T> return type. ActionResult implements IActionResult, so semantically it's an IActionResult helper. I'll document it clearly.

Wait, but actually — hmm, let me reconsider whether I can satisfy the literal `IActionResult` requirement AND the one-liner requirement for ActionResult<T> endpoints.

What if the helper is:
```csharp
private IActionResult Execute(Func<IActionResult> body) { ... }
```

And Browse is:
```csharp

    => Execute(() => Ok(_service.Browse(path ?? string.Empty)));
```

This fails to compile (no IActionResult → ActionResult<T> conversion).

So it's literally impossible to satisfy both the `IActionResult` helper return type AND have Browse be a clean one-liner returning ActionResult<T>. 

Given the conflict, the task's PRIMARY goal (dedup + one-liner delegation) must win over the literal `IActionResult` typing. I'll use `ActionResult`.

Hold on, let me reconsider ONE more time. Let me re-read the exact task text:

> - `private IActionResult Execute(Func<IActionResult> body)` — synchronous endpoints.

> Browse → `Execute(() => Ok(_service.Browse(path ?? string.Empty)))`

The task explicitly wants `IActionResult`. But this is technically impossible with ActionResult<T> return types and clean one-liners...

IActionResult → ActionResult: no implicit conversion. So `return body()` fails. BUT, I could do `return (ActionResult)body();`. Since at runtime body() returns an ActionResult-derived type, the cast works. But that's an ugly cast in the helper.

Alternatively, `Func<ActionResult>` delegate: then body() returns ActionResult, and `return body()` works, and Ok(...)/BadRequest(...) are assignable to ActionResult. Clean.

content.thinking:
With `ActionResult` typed helper and `Func<ActionResult>` delegate, both `ActionResult<T>` endpoints AND `IActionResult` endpoints work as clean one-liners. `ActionResult` implements `IActionResult`, so semantically it's an IActionResult helper.

Now, the task literally says `IActionResult Execute(Func<IActionResult>)`. But that doesn't compile with the ActionResult<T> endpoints. So I'll use `ActionResult` which:
1. Achieves the dedup goal (one-liner delegation)
2. Compiles cleanly
3. `ActionResult` IS-A `IActionResult`, so it's spiritually the same
4. All tests pass

Actually wait. Let me reconsider one more time. The task is VERY explicit. Let me see if there's a way to honor `IActionResult` while still getting clean one-liners.

What if the helper returns IActionResult but I add a SECOND tiny helper that converts for ActionResult<T>? No, too complex.

What if Browse/Search are NOT one-liners but call Execute and the conversion is done? E.g.:
```csharp
public ActionResult<BrowseResultDto> Browse([FromQuery] string? path)
{
    return Execute(() => Ok(_service.Browse(path ?? string.Empty)));
}
```
Still fails because Execute returns IActionResult.

Hmm, what about:
```csharp
public ActionResult<BrowseResultDto> Browse([FromQuery] string? path)
{
    return (ActionResult)Execute(() => Ok(_service.Browse(path ?? string.Empty)));
}
```

```csharp
private async Task<ActionResult> ExecuteAsync(Func<Task<ActionResult>> body)
{
    try
    {
        return await body();
    }
    catch (ArgumentException ex) { ... }
    // no InvalidOperationException catch
}
```

When body() throws InvalidOperationException (synchronously from FakeFileService.UploadAsync before returning a task, but since the lambda is async, it's captured into the task), then `await body()` rethrows InvalidOperationException. The catch blocks don't match. So it propagates out of ExecuteAsync, faulting the returned Task<ActionResult>. Then Upload awaits ExecuteAsync, so it propagates out of Upload, faulting Task<IActionResult>. Test: `await Assert.ThrowsAsync<InvalidOperationException>(() => controller.Upload(...))`. ✓

So it propagates out of Execute, out of Browse. Test: `Assert.Throws<InvalidOperationException>(() => controller.Browse("any"))`. ✓

```csharp
    => Execute(() => Ok(_service.Search(query ?? string.Empty, path ?? string.Empty)));
```

For Upload (async, multi-statement lambda):
```csharp
[HttpPost("upload")]
public async Task<IActionResult> Upload([FromQuery] string? path, [FromForm] IFormFile file)
    => await ExecuteAsync(async () =>
    {
        await _service.UploadAsync(path ?? string.Empty, file);
        var dir = NormalizeRelativePath(path ?? string.Empty);
        var resultPath = string.IsNullOrEmpty(dir) ? file.FileName : $"{dir}/{file.FileName}";
        return Ok(new { path = resultPath });
    });
```

For Download (multi-statement lambda):
```csharp
[HttpGet("download")]
public IActionResult Download([FromQuery] string path)
    => Execute(() =>
    {
        var full = _service.ResolveFullPath(path);
        var contentType = ContentTypeProvider.TryGetContentType(full, out var ct)
            ? ct
            : "application/octet-stream";
        return PhysicalFile(full, contentType, Path.GetFileName(full));
    });
```

For Delete:
```csharp
[HttpDelete("delete")]
public IActionResult Delete([FromQuery] string path)
    => Execute(() =>
    {
        _service.Delete(path);
        return Ok(new { success = true });
    });
```

For Move:
```csharp
[HttpPost("move")]
public IActionResult Move([FromBody] MoveRequest request)
    => Execute(() =>
    {
        _service.Move(request);
        return Ok(new { success = true });
    });
```

```csharp
[HttpPost("copy")]
public IActionResult Copy([FromBody] CopyRequest request)
    => Execute(() =>
    {
        _service.Copy(request);
        return Ok(new { success = true });
    });
```

## Summary

I extracted the duplicated 5-catch block in `Controllers/FilesController.cs` into two private helpers (`Execute` and `ExecuteAsync`), so each of the seven endpoints becomes a one-line delegation.

**What changed:**
- Added `private ActionResult Execute(Func<ActionResult> body)` — wraps a synchronous delegate and performs the identical `ArgumentException`/`UnauthorizedAccessException`/`DirectoryNotFoundException`/`FileNotFoundException`/`IOException` → `BadRequest(new { error = ex.Message })` translation; all other exceptions propagate.
- Added `private async Task<ActionResult> ExecuteAsync(Func<Task<ActionResult>> body)` — async variant for the Upload endpoint. The body is `await`ed (never `.Result`/`.Wait()`), so a faulting body surfaces its original exception rather than an `AggregateException`.
- Refactored Browse, Search, Upload, Download, Delete, Move, Copy to delegate as specified. Duplicated catch boilerplate dropped from 35 clauses (7 endpoints × 5) to 10 (5 per helper × 2 helpers).
- Preserved every XML doc comment, the `NormalizeRelativePath` helper, the `ContentTypeProvider`, section banners, and all observable behavior (status codes, `{error}`/`{success}`/`{path}` payload shapes, exact exception mapping, non-translated-exception propagation, single-invocation).

**One deviation from the literal spec (documented in the helper's `<remarks>`):** the helpers return `ActionResult` (and take `Func<ActionResult>` / `Func<Task<ActionResult>>`) rather than the spec's `IActionResult`. I empirically confirmed `ActionResult<T>` has **no** implicit conversion from `IActionResult` (compiler error CS0029 on .NET 10), so an `IActionResult`-typed helper cannot back the Browse/Search endpoints — which must keep their `ActionResult<T>` return types because `FilesControllerTests.cs` accesses `.Result` on them. `ActionResult` implements `IActionResult` (so it's behaviorally identical), and it converts implicitly to `ActionResult<T>`, letting every endpoint be a clean one-liner. This was the only way to honor the task's "each endpoint becomes a one-line delegation" requirement.

**Verification:** `dotnet build TestProject.sln` → 0 warnings, 0 errors. `dotnet test TestProject.Tests/TestProject.Tests.csproj` → **287 passed, 0 failed** (unchanged from before; 168 FilesControllerTests, all passing without modification).
